claim: Medical domain speech recognition requires <10% word error rate and <500ms latency due to patient safety and cost constraints
actionable: If building healthcare speech systems, set WER targets below 10% and latency below 500ms from the start; these are non-negotiable constraints
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: Transformers + distillation is the best approach for domain-specific speech models, not starting with large models
actionable: Start ASR development with transformer-based models, then distill them down to LSTM-sized models for production latency and cost
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: For domain-specific models, quality beats quantity; a few hundred expert-verified samples outperform thousands of unverified samples
actionable: When fine-tuning speech models, invest in expert verification and curation from day one rather than scraping large amounts of noisy data
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: Use 70-80% real data and 20-30% synthetic augmentation for speech model training, not the reverse
actionable: When augmenting speech data, cap synthetic data at 20-30% because TTS doesn't mimic acoustic properties well enough to dominate training
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: Human side-by-side evaluation (0-5 ratings) should start from day one, not after training is complete
actionable: Set up human evaluation rubrics and rating systems at the start of model development; adding them later requires restarting from scratch
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: Modeling is only 30% of effort; the remaining 70% is pipelines, monitoring, scaling, and infrastructure
actionable: Allocate engineering effort as 30% model work, 70% everything else—pipelines, deployment, monitoring, and scaling matter most
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: Start model selection with medium-sized pretrained models and grid search on hyperparameters before trying large models
actionable: Begin ASR experiments with medium models (Whisper-medium, medium-sized transformers), use grid search for hyperparameters, scale up only if needed
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: Foundation models exist to avoid learning representations multiple times; they enable cross-domain transfer and faster experimentation
actionable: Build or use foundation models for your domain to enable fast downstream task development and transfer learning across similar tasks
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: Attention weights are not causation; high-attention features don't guarantee importance to the task outcome
actionable: Don't assume high attention = importance; use ablation studies to verify that removing high-attention features actually hurts performance
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: LLM-as-judge is empirically validated (80%+ agreement with human evaluators) and is now the industry default for agent evaluation
actionable: Use LLM-as-judge for large-scale agent evaluation, but periodically validate against humans and rebuild rubrics when drift occurs
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: LLM judges suffer from positional bias and verbosity bias; use round-robin, ensemble judges, and exact-length instructions to mitigate
actionable: When using LLM judges, rotate response order, use multiple judges as an ensemble, and instruct judges to provide brief fixed-length responses
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: Traditional NLP metrics (BLEU, ROUGE) fail for LLM outputs because they don't capture multi-step agentic workflows and tool complexity
actionable: Don't use BLEU/ROUGE for evaluating LLM agents; instead use LLM-as-judge or human evaluation with task-specific rubrics
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: MLflow GenAI provides scalable multi-model agent evaluation with tracing, prompt registry, and gateway control in a unified interface
actionable: Use MLflow GenAI's evaluation module to define rubrics, run LLM judges, and track prompt versions in a single platform
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation

claim: 2025 is the decade of agentic systems; building agents is easy now, but evaluating them rigorously remains the hard part
actionable: Invest heavily in evaluation infrastructure and rubrics; don't optimize for quick agent builds without corresponding evaluation rigor
source_title: Building AI Speech Stacks, Foundational Models & LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation
